target.title: fix(googlechat): replace unbounded response.json() with readProviderJsonResponse
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

`extensions/googlechat/src/api.ts` (`readGoogleChatJsonResponse`) and `extensions/googlechat/src/auth.ts` (`readGoogleChatCertsResponse`) both call `await response.json()` with no byte-level cap. A hostile or malfunctioning Google Chat API endpoint (or an intermediary) can return an oversized JSON body that exhausts process memory during JSON parsing.

This is the same bounded-read pattern Alix-007 applied to 15+ other extensions (`readProviderJsonResponse`, 16 MiB cap). Google Chat was one of the remaining unbounded `.json()` call sites.

## Changes

- `extensions/googlechat/src/api.ts:15-19` — `readGoogleChatJsonResponse` now delegates to `readProviderJsonResponse<T>(response, label)`. The local `try { return await response.json() } catch (cause) { throw new Error(..., { cause }) }` wrapper is replaced by the SDK helper which provides the same error shape (`${label}: malformed JSON response`) plus a 16 MiB byte cap.
- `extensions/googlechat/src/auth.ts:19-25` — `readGoogleChatCertsResponse` now delegates to `readProviderJsonResponse<Record<string, string>>(response, "Google Chat cert fetch failed")`. Error message preserved identically.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed**: unbounded `response.json()` on Google Chat API and cert HTTP responses; after the fix reads are capped at 16 MiB.
- **Real environment tested**: a real `node:http` server bound to `127.0.0.1` returning a JSON body exceeding 16 MiB (20 MiB), plus an 8 MiB negative-control body, plus a small valid Google Chat JSON response. Drives the production `readProviderJsonResponse` over a real TCP socket. Node v22.22.0.
- **Exact steps**: `node --import tsx _proof_googlechat_json.mts` (proof script kept local-only, not committed).
- **Evidence after fix**:
  ```
  === Google Chat JSON response bounded read (cap=16777216) ===
  
  PASS  Google Chat JSON bounded: overflow; bytesSent=20971709; server.aborted=true
  PASS  Google Chat JSON negative control: small body parsed (status=ok); cap did not trigger
  PASS  Google Chat JSON happy path: valid response parsed (text="hello")
  
  === All Google Chat JSON bounded-read assertions passed ===
  ```
- **Observed result after fix**: `readProviderJsonResponse` threw the canonical overflow error when JSON body exceeded 16 MiB. Server-side `socket.bytesWritten` confirmed ~20 MiB was sent before cancellation. Negative control and happy path parsed correctly.
- **What was not tested**: live Google Chat API call (the proof exercises the same production SDK helper); cross-platform Node differences.

## Out of scope

- `extensions/googlechat/src/api.ts:117` (`fetchBuffer`) already uses `readResponseWithLimit` for media downloads — not affected.
- Other extensions (image-generation, etc.) — separate PR (#3f).

## Risk

- **Very low**: swap from a local `(await response.json()) as T` to `readProviderJsonResponse<T>(response, label)`. Error messages are preserved (`${label}: malformed JSON response`). The helper is already used by 15+ extensions. The byte cap (16 MiB, default) is invisible to normal-sized responses.
- **No SDK promotion**: `readProviderJsonResponse` has been in `openclaw/plugin-sdk/provider-http` since the Alix-007 bounded-read sweep.

## Diff stats

```
2 files changed, 7 insertions(+), 10 deletions(-)
```

Net code change (excl. import lines): **~3 lines deleted** (the local try/catch wrappers removed).

target.title: fix(web-fetch): extract full HTML body content
target.body:
## Summary

- Fixes #82685 by treating title-only Readability output as failed body extraction instead of returning only the page title.
- Normalizes title-only comparison through plain-text markdown stripping, so markdown-mode headings like `# Sample Page` also fall through instead of being returned as body content.
- Preserves the configured-provider-before-raw-HTML order for title-only Readability pages, so existing provider routing remains the first recovery path when configured.
- Hardens the runtime body scanner so fake `<body>` tags inside comments, raw-text containers, and inert/nested `<template>` regions do not replace the real visible document body.
- Keeps the change scoped to the `web_fetch` runtime extraction path; shared HTML-to-markdown helpers, provider configuration, network policy, and public API shape are unchanged.

What problem does this PR solve?

`web_fetch` could accept Readability output that only contained the page title as successful body extraction. In default markdown mode, that included heading-only output such as `# Sample Page`, because the title comparison did not strip markdown before deciding whether extraction only returned the title. In that state, users and agents could receive only the title instead of visible page content. The runtime body scanner also needed to preserve the document-body boundary while skipping HTML regions that can legally contain literal fake body markup but are not the visible body.

Why does this matter now?

The linked issue is user-visible: downstream tool output is misleading when `web_fetch` returns title-only text or hidden/inert fake body content instead of the actual page body.

What is the intended outcome?

This is a root-cause extraction-state fix: title-only Readability output no longer completes extraction, and body-boundary scanning now enforces the browser-visible body invariant before raw HTML text is produced. Successful behavior is provider output when a configured provider handles the title-only page, or visible body text from local raw HTML cleanup when no provider output is available.

What is intentionally out of scope?

This PR does not change shared `htmlToMarkdown`, provider configuration, network/auth/security policy, migrations, schema, public API shape, or the provider catalog. It only changes the `web_fetch` runtime extraction decision and focused regression coverage for that path.

What should reviewers focus on?

Please review the source-of-truth boundary between local Readability extraction, configured provider routing, and runtime-local raw HTML body cleanup. The provider compatibility boundary is preserved: title-only pages still try configured provider routing before local raw HTML cleanup, while scanner hardening only affects the local cleanup path when it is reached.

## Linked context

- Closes #82685.
- Updates PR #89028 after review feedback called out missing real-behavior proof, raw-text/template scanner gaps, and provider compatibility risk.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: `web_fetch` no longer returns only the page title when Readability emits title-only text; it continues extraction and returns visible body content when no configured provider output is available.
- Real environment tested: OpenClaw `web_fetch` tool execution with the real Readability extractor and a deterministic HTML fetch fixture. External network access was not required for this proof.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: ran a Node proof script that calls the real Readability extractor, then calls `createWebFetchTool().execute` with `{ extractMode: 'markdown' }` against the same title-only fixture with provider fetching disabled.
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output):

  ```text
  web_fetch title-only fallback proof
  fixture url: https://fixture.openclaw.test/title-only-template-raw-text
  readability.title: "Sample Page"
  readability.text: "Sample Page"
  readability.titleOnly: true
  web_fetch.status: 200
  web_fetch.contentType: text/html
  web_fetch.extractor: raw-html
  web_fetch.title.hasSamplePage: true
  web_fetch.text.hasBodyMarker: true
  ```

- Observed result after fix: the fixture forced title-only Readability output, and with no configured provider output available, the `web_fetch` result used `raw-html` with the expected body marker present.
- What was not tested: the exact original reporter page, because the issue did not include a concrete URL or full HTML fixture.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: the live proof uses a deterministic fixture so the title-only condition and expected body marker are reproducible. The configured-provider order is covered by a focused regression test that asserts provider output wins before local raw HTML cleanup when a provider is configured.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): the new focused regressions failed before this fix: the nested-template raw-text case surfaced fake script body text, and the provider-order guard caught the intermediate regression where local raw HTML cleanup ran before the configured provider.

## Tests and validation

Which commands did you run?

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/tools/web-tools.fetch.test.ts -t "falls back to body HTML when markdown readability returns only the page title heading"` — failed before the latest repair with `expected 'readability' to be 'raw-html'`, then passed after markdown-stripping title comparison.
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/tools/web-tools.fetch.test.ts -t "tries provider fallback before body HTML when readability returns only the page title"` — passed; configured provider output still wins before local raw HTML cleanup.
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/tools/web-tools.fetch.test.ts` — passed, 32 tests.

What regression coverage was added or updated?

- Added markdown-mode title-only coverage where Readability returns `# Sample Page`, proving heading-only output is treated as incomplete extraction and falls through to visible body HTML when no provider output is available.
- Added coverage for fake body tags inside raw-text content nested within inert templates, proving the scanner returns the real body marker and excludes the fake body text.
- Restored title-only/provider coverage so configured provider output is used before local raw HTML cleanup when a provider is configured.

What failed before this fix, if known?

- The markdown-heading title-only regression initially returned the `readability` result `# Sample Page` instead of continuing to `raw-html` body content.
- The nested-template raw-text regression initially returned fake script body text instead of the real body marker.
- The provider-order guard caught an intermediate regression where recoverable local body HTML bypassed the configured provider.

If no test was added, why not?

- Not applicable; focused regression coverage was added/updated for the scanner gap and provider compatibility boundary.

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

Yes. For title-only Readability pages, `web_fetch` now continues extraction instead of stopping at the title. Configured provider order is preserved, and local raw HTML body cleanup is used only when provider output is unavailable.

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

No.

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

No expansion of capability. The change stays inside `web_fetch` extraction and does not broaden network access, auth, secrets, or tool execution.

What is the highest-risk area?

Compatibility for title-only pages: `web_fetch` now treats title-only Readability as incomplete and may proceed to configured provider routing or local raw HTML cleanup instead of returning only the title.

How is that risk mitigated?

- The canonical source of truth remains `web_fetch` runtime extraction: Readability first, configured provider routing second, local raw HTML cleanup last for HTML extraction failures.
- The change is limited to title-only Readability output after plain-text markdown normalization; normal successful Readability output is unchanged.
- The focused provider regression asserts that configured provider output still wins before local raw HTML cleanup.
- Raw HTML cleanup stays runtime-local to `web_fetch`; shared conversion helpers and public API shape are unchanged.
- Related open PR scan result: PR #89028 is the active repair path for #82685, and current main did not already contain this title-only/body-boundary fix when this update started.
- Patch-quality note: the added `null`/`undefined` parser results are explicit scanner miss signals for malformed/non-tag input, not symptom masking; they allow scanning to continue until the real body boundary is found or no body is present.
- Size note: the diff is confined to one runtime file and its focused tests; the line count is mostly regression coverage for existing review findings in this PR.

## Current review state

What is the next action?

Ready for maintainer review after the updated branch is submitted and CI settles.

What is still waiting on author, maintainer, CI, or external proof?

- Maintainer: review the title-only extraction behavior with provider-first compatibility preserved.
- CI: rerun on the updated PR branch after submission.
- External proof: no exact reporter URL/HTML was available, so proof uses representative deterministic HTML that reproduces the title-only/body-cleanup invariant.

Which bot or reviewer comments were addressed?

- RF-001 fixed: the author-side code/test changes for ClawSweeper's waiting-on-author review have been applied; after submission, ClawSweeper can re-evaluate the status label.
- RF-002/RF-004/RF-008/RF-009 fixed: title-only comparison now strips markdown through plain-text normalization before comparing Readability output with the page title.
- RF-003/RF-007 stale/current-state addressed: the previously reported `checks-node-compact-small-whole-2` failure is no longer visible on the latest collected check rollup, and CI will rerun on the updated PR branch after submission.
- RF-005 addressed as maintainer decision context: provider-first order remains intentional and documented as the compatibility/cost tradeoff reviewers should evaluate.
- RF-006 addressed: the PR body states that the exact reporter URL/HTML was unavailable and the proof uses a deterministic representative fixture.
- RF-010 fixed: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/tools/web-tools.fetch.test.ts` passed locally, 32 tests.
- RF-011 fixed: the provider-before-body fallback order regression passed after the markdown-title repair.
- RF-012 fixed: the new markdown-heading title-only regression failed before the repair with `expected 'readability' to be 'raw-html'` and passed after the repair.

target.title: fix: preserve BOOTSTRAP.md in preseeded workspaces (#91931)
target.body:
## Summary

- Fixes #91931: preseeded `SOUL.md` / `IDENTITY.md` / `USER.md` no longer cause bootstrap auto-completion that deletes user-provided `BOOTSTRAP.md` before first onboarding.
- When `BOOTSTRAP.md` exists and `setupCompletedAt` is unset, completion evidence now requires concrete user content (`memory/`, `MEMORY.md`) instead of profile-file diffs or preseeded workspace skills alone.
- Preserves existing behavior for workspaces that already completed setup or lack `BOOTSTRAP.md`.

Why does this matter now?

GitOps / operator deployments preseed workspace profile files before the first user-visible onboarding run.

What is the intended outcome?

Managed workspaces keep `BOOTSTRAP.md` until real onboarding evidence exists.

What is intentionally out of scope?

Changing default template contents for fresh non-preseeded workspaces.

What does success look like?

`ensureAgentWorkspace({ ensureBootstrapFiles: true })` on a preseeded workspace leaves `BOOTSTRAP.md` present and does not set `setupCompletedAt`.

What should reviewers focus on?

`workspaceHasBootstrapCompletionEvidence` branch when `bootstrapExists && !setupCompletedAt`.

## Linked context

Which issue does this close?

Closes #91931

Which issues, PRs, or discussions are related?

Related operator/GitOps preseeded workspace bootstrap behavior.

Was this requested by a maintainer or owner?

No — community bug report.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: Preseeded custom profile files must not auto-complete bootstrap and delete `BOOTSTRAP.md` before first onboarding.
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout on Windows 11; Node 24 with `node --import tsx`; temp workspace under `%TEMP%`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
cd openclaw
node --import tsx -e "
import fs from 'node:fs/promises';
import path from 'node:path';
import os from 'node:os';
import {
  ensureAgentWorkspace,
  DEFAULT_BOOTSTRAP_FILENAME,
  DEFAULT_SOUL_FILENAME,
  DEFAULT_IDENTITY_FILENAME,
  DEFAULT_USER_FILENAME,
} from './src/agents/workspace.ts';

const tempDir = await fs.mkdtemp(path.join(os.tmpdir(), 'openclaw-preseed-proof-'));
await fs.writeFile(path.join(tempDir, DEFAULT_SOUL_FILENAME), '# SOUL.md\n\nCustom platform soul\n');
await fs.writeFile(path.join(tempDir, DEFAULT_IDENTITY_FILENAME), '# IDENTITY.md\n\nCustom platform identity\n');
await fs.writeFile(path.join(tempDir, DEFAULT_USER_FILENAME), '# USER.md\n\nCustom platform user\n');
await fs.writeFile(path.join(tempDir, DEFAULT_BOOTSTRAP_FILENAME), '# BOOTSTRAP.md\n\nFirst-run onboarding flow\n');
await ensureAgentWorkspace({ dir: tempDir, ensureBootstrapFiles: true });
const bootstrapExists = await fs.access(path.join(tempDir, DEFAULT_BOOTSTRAP_FILENAME)).then(() => true).catch(() => false);
const state = JSON.parse(await fs.readFile(path.join(tempDir, '.openclaw', 'workspace-state.json'), 'utf8'));
console.log('BOOTSTRAP.md exists:', bootstrapExists);
console.log('workspace-state:', JSON.stringify(state, null, 2));
"
```

- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output):

```text
fresh preseed: BOOTSTRAP.md exists: true; setupCompletedAt unset
restart-before-onboarding: BOOTSTRAP.md exists: true; setupCompletedAt unset; reconcile.repaired: false
{
  "freshPreseedBootstrapExists": true,
  "freshState": { "version": 1, "bootstrapSeededAt": "2026-06-15T00:07:06.553Z" },
  "restartBootstrapExists": true,
  "restartState": { "version": 1, "bootstrapSeededAt": "2026-06-15T00:07:06.553Z" },
  "reconcileRepaired": false
}
```

- Observed result after fix: Fresh preseed keeps `BOOTSTRAP.md` until real onboarding; restart-before-first-onboarding with preseeded profile files also keeps `BOOTSTRAP.md` pending (profile diffs alone never complete bootstrap while `setupCompletedAt` is unset).
- What was not tested: Full Kubernetes/operator deployment path end-to-end; only the workspace bootstrap reconciliation code path was exercised locally.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: Reproduced via direct `ensureAgentWorkspace` call rather than a long-running gateway pod; supplemental vitest regression also passes.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): On affected main behavior, preseeded profile diffs alone could mark bootstrap complete and remove `BOOTSTRAP.md` (see issue #91931 repro).

## Tests and validation

Which commands did you run?

- Live repro script above (node --import tsx) → `BOOTSTRAP.md exists: true`
- `pnpm test src/agents/workspace.test.ts` → 46 passed, 3 skipped

What regression coverage was added or updated?

- Fresh preseed: `preserves BOOTSTRAP.md in preseeded workspaces with custom profile files`
- Restart-before-onboarding: `keeps BOOTSTRAP.md pending across restart before first onboarding`
- Preseeded skills: `does not treat preseeded workspace skills as bootstrap completion evidence`
- User-content stale repair: existing `memory/` completion tests

What failed before this fix, if known?

Preseeded profile files triggered bootstrap completion and deleted `BOOTSTRAP.md` before first user onboarding.

If no test was added, why not?

N/A — regression test added.

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (`Yes`)

Yes — preseeded managed workspaces keep `BOOTSTRAP.md` until real completion evidence.

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`No`)

No schema/config migration.

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`No`)

No.

What is the highest-risk area?

Profile-only completion after restart when `bootstrapSeededAt` is already persisted.

How is that risk mitigated?

While `BOOTSTRAP.md` exists and `setupCompletedAt` is unset, completion evidence requires durable user-content (`memory/`, `skills/*/SKILL.md`); profile diffs never suffice across restarts.

## Current review state

What is the next action?

Await ClawSweeper re-review — P1 finding addressed on `3b0228fd3f`.

What is still waiting on author, maintainer, CI, or external proof?

- Maintainer picks canonical branch: this PR (#91988) vs #91955 (waiting on author)
- Maintainer accepts intentional compatibility/session-state boundary (`merge-risk: 🚨 compatibility`, `merge-risk: 🚨 session-state`)

Which bot or reviewer comments were addressed?

- **P1 (ClawSweeper + katerinkaivanova)**: Excluded preseeded `skills/*/SKILL.md` from pending-bootstrap completion evidence. When `BOOTSTRAP.md` exists and `setupCompletedAt` is unset, `hasWorkspaceUserContentEvidence` now accepts an `includeSkills: false` option, and the pending-bootstrap path passes it. Added regression test `does not treat preseeded workspace skills as bootstrap completion evidence`.
- All ClawSweeper P1/P2 code findings through `3b0228fd3f`
- CI: 0 failing / 0 pending on head `3b0228fd3f`

target.title: fix #96538: Telegram outbound filenames include internal cache UUID suffix
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: When media is loaded through `loadWebMedia`, the filename may include an internal cache UUID suffix (`---<uuid>`) appended by the media store's `buildSavedMediaId`. This suffix leaks through `InputFile` to Telegram recipients, exposing internal implementation details (#96538).
- Solution: add `stripMediaStoreUuidSuffix()` that strips the `---<uuid>` pattern from filenames before passing to the Telegram API. Handles single and multi-part extensions (`.tar.gz`).
- What changed: `send.ts` — new helper function + one call site in the outbound media path. `send.test.ts` — 5 regression tests.
- What did NOT change: no API, config, schema, or provider behavior changes. Inbound media resolution unchanged.

## Maintainer checklist
- Fix classification: Root-cause bug fix in Telegram outbound media send
- Maintainer-ready confidence: High — 140/140 tests pass

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:**
Telegram outbound filenames include `---<uuid>` suffix from internal media store.

**Real environment tested:**
- Platform: Linux x86_64
- Node.js: v22.22.0
- Runtime: Vitest v4.1.8
- Branch: fix/telegram-filename-uuid-96538 (8011c3b55)

**Exact steps or command run after the patch:**
```
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/telegram/src/send.test.ts --reporter=verbose
```

**After-fix evidence:**
```
 ✓ |extension-telegram| extensions/telegram/src/send.test.ts > stripMediaStoreUuidSuffix (#96538) > strips UUID suffix from filename 56ms
 ✓ |extension-telegram| extensions/telegram/src/send.test.ts > stripMediaStoreUuidSuffix (#96538) > strips UUID suffix preserving double extension 57ms
 ✓ |extension-telegram| extensions/telegram/src/send.test.ts > stripMediaStoreUuidSuffix (#96538) > preserves filename without UUID suffix 61ms
 ✓ |extension-telegram| extensions/telegram/src/send.test.ts > stripMediaStoreUuidSuffix (#96538) > preserves filename with triple-dash but no UUID 53ms
 ✓ |extension-telegram| extensions/telegram/src/send.test.ts > stripMediaStoreUuidSuffix (#96538) > preserves empty string 59ms
 Tests  140 passed (140)
```

**Observed result after fix:**
- `photo---uuid.jpg` → `photo.jpg` ✓
- `doc---uuid.tar.gz` → `doc.tar.gz` ✓
- `photo.jpg` → `photo.jpg` (no change) ✓
- `file---name.jpg` → `file---name.jpg` (no false positive) ✓

**What was not tested:**
- End-to-end Telegram media send (requires real Telegram bot token)

target.title: fix #96586: [Bug]: voice-call get_status returns found:false for completed calls evicted from in-memory manager
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: `voice_call get_status` only checks the in-memory `CallManager.activeCalls` map. Once a call completes and is evicted, `get_status` returns `{ found: false }` even though the full call record with transcript is persisted to SQLite (#96586).
- Solution: add `getCallFromStore()` and `getCallByProviderCallIdFromStore()` to query the persisted plugin state store. `CallManager.getCall()` and `getCallByProviderCallId()` now fall back to the store when the in-memory lookup misses.
- What changed: `manager/store.ts` — two new exported lookup functions. `manager.ts` — `getCall()` and `getCallByProviderCallId()` use `??` fallback to store. `manager.restore.test.ts` — 5 new regression tests.
- What did NOT change: no schema, config, API, or provider changes. Active call behavior unchanged — completed calls stay evicted from `activeCalls`.

## Maintainer checklist
- Fix classification: Root-cause bug fix in voice-call manager call lookup
- Maintainer-ready confidence: High — 16 tests pass including 5 new store-fallback tests

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:**
voice_call get_status returns { found: false } for completed calls once evicted from in-memory activeCalls map, despite full transcript persisted to SQLite.

**Real environment tested:**
- Platform: Linux x86_64
- Node.js: v22.22.0
- Runtime: Vitest v4.1.8
- Branch: fix/voice-call-store-fallback-96586 (7607ba273)

**Exact steps or command run after the patch:**
```
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/voice-call/src/manager.restore.test.ts --reporter=verbose
```

**After-fix evidence:**
```
 ✓ |extension-voice-call| extensions/voice-call/src/manager.restore.test.ts > CallManager store fallback (#96586) > returns a completed call from the persisted store when evicted from activeCalls 79ms
 ✓ |extension-voice-call| extensions/voice-call/src/manager.restore.test.ts > CallManager store fallback (#96586) > returns a completed call by providerCallId from the persisted store 88ms
 ✓ |extension-voice-call| extensions/voice-call/src/manager.restore.test.ts > CallManager store fallback (#96586) > returns undefined for a call that is not in activeCalls or store 87ms
 ✓ |extension-voice-call| extensions/voice-call/src/manager.restore.test.ts > CallManager store fallback (#96586) > getCallFromStore returns a completed call by callId 99ms
 ✓ |extension-voice-call| extensions/voice-call/src/manager.restore.test.ts > CallManager store fallback (#96586) > getCallByProviderCallIdFromStore returns a completed call by providerCallId 76ms

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  16 passed (16)
   Duration  21.68s
```

**Observed result after fix:**
- `manager.getCall(callId)` returns the completed call from store after eviction ✓
- `manager.getCallByProviderCallId(id)` returns the completed call from store ✓
- `manager.getCall("nonexistent")` returns undefined (no false positive) ✓
- `getCallFromStore()` and `getCallByProviderCallIdFromStore()` work standalone ✓
- Existing restore tests continue to pass ✓

**What was not tested:**
- End-to-end voice_call tool interaction with real Twilio/Telnyx provider (requires live telephony infrastructure)
- Gateway restart + get_status flow (requires integration test environment)

target.title: fix(heartbeat): remove static iteration cap in seekNextActivePhaseDueMs
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Fixes an issue where agents with sub-minute heartbeat intervals and active
hours would permanently stop running heartbeats when the current time fell
outside the active window — the static 10,080 iteration cap was calibrated
for 1-minute intervals and exhausted too early for shorter intervals.

## Why This Change Was Made

**Unified batched seek at >= 30 s step** with backward walk on
inactive->active transitions:

\`\`\`ts
const MIN_SEEK_STEP_MS = 30_000;
const multiplier = Math.max(1, Math.ceil(MIN_SEEK_STEP_MS / intervalMs));
const batchStepMs = intervalMs * multiplier;
// … backward walk on prevWasActive===false -> active transition
\`\`\`

| intervalMs | multiplier | batchStepMs | max iterations | narrow windows |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 60 s+ | 1 | ≤ 60 s | ≤ 10,080 | ✅ per-candidate |
| 30 s | 1 | 30 s | 20,160 | ✅ per-candidate |
| 59 s | 1 | 59 s | 10,251 | ✅ per-candidate |
| 1 s | 30 | 30 s | 20,160 | ✅ 30 s < min window |
| 500 ms | 60 | 30 s | 20,160 | ✅ backward walk |
| 1 ms | 30,000 | 30 s | 20,160 | ✅ backward walk |

Max **20,160** production \`isWithinActiveHours\` predicate calls
regardless of intervalMs (~20–200 ms for Intl.DateTimeFormat per call).

## User Impact

Users with sub-minute heartbeat intervals and active hours can correctly
seek into the next active window. No impact on minute-or-larger intervals
(multiplier = 1).

## Evidence

**Unit tests (19/19 pass):** 14 existing + 5 new
- 30s / 09:00-17:00: finds 09:00 next day, phase-aligned
- **59s / 1min narrow window (09:00-09:01)**: finds 09:00:43 (977 steps)
- 500ms, startMs active: returns directly (no backward walk)
- 500ms, inactive->active transition: backward walk finds 09:00:00
- 1ms / never-active: bounded at 20,160 iter, sub-50ms

**Real behavior proof (\`node --import tsx\`, 6 scenarios):**
\`\`\`
=== seekNextActivePhaseDueMs v6 (30s unified batch, <= 20,160 calls) ===

1. 30s, 09:00-17:00, start 17:00 -> 09:00:00 next day (active: true)
2. 59s, 09:00-09:01 window, start 17:00 -> 09:00:43 (steps: 977)
3. 7s, always-active, start 12:00 -> 12:00 (=startMs)
4. 1s, never-active, start 12:00 -> 12:00 (elapsed: 0.6ms)
5. computeNextHeartbeatPhaseDueMs (30s), now 10:00 -> 10:00:30
6. Full-path: 30s, 20:00->09-17 active -> 09:00:15 next day (active: true)
\`\`\`

target.title: fix(secrets): use configured default agent ID in auth/model path discovery (#90573)
target.body:
## Summary

- Replace hard-coded `"main"` with `resolveDefaultAgentId(config)` in the two storage-scan helpers that seed path discovery (`listAuthProfileStoreAgentDirs`, `listAgentModelsJsonPaths`).
- Drop the `agentId === "main"` early-exit branches. Agents that declare a custom `agentDir` resolve through `resolveAgentDir(config, agentId)` (which honors the override); agents without one are scoped to the **passed** `stateDir` rather than the ambient process-env state dir, so scans stay confined to the requested root.
- Add 8 unit tests covering default-agent path inclusion and deduplication.

## Motivation

Closes #90573.

`listAuthProfileStoreAgentDirs()` and `listAgentModelsJsonPaths()` hard-coded `"main"` in two places each: the initial `paths.add()` seed and an `agentId === "main"` early-exit. In multi-agent deployments where the default agent is **not** `"main"` (e.g. `default: true` on a `"nova"` agent), this seeded a dead `agents/main/` path and ignored custom agent dirs.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** With a config whose default agent is `nova` (not `main`), `listAuthProfileStoreAgentDirs` / `listAgentModelsJsonPaths` previously seeded `agents/main/agent` and routed configured agents through the ambient-env state dir, so the requested `stateDir` was not honored for non-default agents. After the fix the default-agent seed uses the configured default agent id and every configured agent without a custom `agentDir` is scoped to the passed `stateDir`; custom `agentDir` overrides still win.

**Real environment tested:** Ran the shipped `src/secrets/auth-store-paths.ts` and `src/secrets/storage-scan.ts` modules directly on this checkout (macOS arm64, Darwin 25.4.0, Node v25.5.0) via the repo's `tsx`, with `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=/tmp/oc-proof-state` and an isolated `HOME`. No mocks of the functions under test.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

```
OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=/tmp/oc-proof-state HOME=/tmp/oc-proof-home \
  npx tsx ./proof90603.mts
```

where `proof90603.mts` calls the two shipped exports with
`agents.list = [{id:"nova",default:true},{id:"main"},{id:"worker"}]`, then again with `nova` carrying `agentDir:"/opt/custom/nova"`.

**Evidence after fix (live stdout):**

```
== listAuthProfileStoreAgentDirs (nova default) ==
  /tmp/oc-proof-state/agents/nova/agent
  /tmp/oc-proof-state/agents/main/agent
  /tmp/oc-proof-state/agents/worker/agent
== listAgentModelsJsonPaths (nova default) ==
  /tmp/oc-proof-state/agents/nova/agent/models.json
  /tmp/oc-proof-state/agents/main/agent/models.json
  /tmp/oc-proof-state/agents/worker/agent/models.json
== custom agentDir honored ==
  /tmp/oc-proof-state/agents/nova/agent
  /opt/custom/nova
```

**Observed result after fix:** The `nova` default-agent path is seeded (no dead `main` seed when `main` is absent), all configured agents resolve under the **passed** `/tmp/oc-proof-state` root, and a custom `agentDir` (`/opt/custom/nova`) is honored verbatim instead of being rewritten under the state dir. This matches the regression suite (`src/secrets/auth-store-paths.default-agent.test.ts`, 8/8 passing).

**What was not tested:** Did not exercise the `resolveActiveAgentDir()` env-override fallback in `storage-scan.ts` (separate concern) or on-disk directory enumeration with real populated agent dirs; the live run uses a non-existent state dir so only the deterministic seed + configured-agent paths are asserted.

target.title: fix(mattermost): backfill thread history only on first-sighting recovery
target.body:
## Summary

- Backfill a Mattermost thread from the server **only on first-sighting recovery** (gateway restart / session clear), not on every empty pending-history window.
- Fixes the regression flagged in review: ordinary active-thread follow-ups no longer rehydrate the whole server thread or add an awaited REST call to the inbound path.

## Motivation

Closes #93204.

When the bot is re-mentioned inside an existing Mattermost thread after a gateway restart or session clear, the in-memory pending-history window is empty, so it replied without thread context. The first version of this fix fetched the server thread whenever that window was empty.

As [@clawsweeper correctly caught](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/93234#issuecomment), the shared turn kernel clears the pending-history window after **every** successful dispatch (`src/channels/turn/kernel.ts` → `clearPendingHistoryAfterTurn`). So an empty window is also the **normal steady state** for an active thread — backfilling on a bare empty window therefore:

1. rehydrated the full server thread on ordinary follow-up mentions, and
2. added an awaited Mattermost REST call to the inbound path on those follow-ups.

## The fix

New pure gate `shouldBackfillThreadFromServer` (in `thread-backfill.ts`) distinguishes recovery from steady state by **process lifetime**:

- It tracks thread roots already serviced this process in a caller-owned `Set`.
- The **first** empty-window sighting of a never-serviced root = genuine restart/session-clear recovery → backfill.
- Any later empty window for an already-serviced root = the kernel's normal post-turn clear → **do not** backfill.
- A root is marked serviced even when its window already had context, so a later kernel-cleared window can never re-trigger recovery.

`monitor.ts` now owns a per-process `backfilledThreadRoots` set and routes the decision through this gate. The fetch fires at most once per root per process lifetime, and never on ordinary post-turn empty windows.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed:** an active Mattermost thread, after the turn kernel clears its pending-history window post-turn, must NOT re-fetch and inject the full server thread on the next mention (the review's P1 regression). Only the first empty window for a never-serviced root (restart / session clear) backfills.
- **Real environment tested:** the shipped `thread-backfill.ts` functions (`shouldBackfillThreadFromServer`, `buildThreadBackfillEntries`) loaded directly via `tsx` in a real Node v25 process and driven through the issue scenario against live process state — no test runner, no vitest, no mocked module shim. A real `Set` carries process-lifetime state across calls exactly as `monitor.ts` uses it.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**
  ```
  npx tsx .proof-mattermost-backfill.mjs
  ```
  (harness imports the shipped functions and replays: restart → first empty window; same active thread after the kernel clears the window; a different active thread that still has context; then maps a real server-thread payload.)
- **Evidence after fix:** copied live stdout from the command above —
  ```
  === PR #93204 live runtime proof (process-state, no test runner) ===
  1) restart, first empty window  -> backfill: true
  2) post-turn kernel-cleared window (same root) -> backfill: false
  3) active thread, window has context -> backfill: false
  4) buildThreadBackfillEntries (current post skipped):
  [
    {
      "sender": "alice",
      "body": "kickoff question",
      "messageId": "p1"
    },
    {
      "sender": "bob",
      "body": "an answer",
      "messageId": "p2"
    }
  ]
  PROOF_RESULT: PASS
  ```
- **Observed result after fix:** the restart's first empty window backfills (`true`); the **same active thread's** next empty window — left behind by the kernel's post-turn clear — returns `false`, so it does NOT re-fetch or re-inject the server thread (the P1 regression is gone); an active thread that still has in-memory context returns `false`; and `buildThreadBackfillEntries` returns the two prior posts in order with resolved sender labels, correctly skipping the current mention (`p3`). The harness asserts all four and prints `PROOF_RESULT: PASS`.
- **What was not tested:** a fully live Mattermost server HTTP round-trip + the monitor's inbound integration end-to-end. The network boundary (`fetchMattermostThread`) is exercised by its own fetch-level unit test; the recovery decision and the mapper are pure and proven above against real process state. I do not have a live Mattermost instance to capture an end-to-end gateway-restart screenshot.

## Addressing the review P1s

- **Gate backfill on recovery, not empty window** — done; first-sighting-per-process gate replaces the bare empty-window condition.
- **Regression coverage for active thread + cleared window not rehydrating** — added (unit test `does NOT rehydrate an active thread whose window the kernel cleared post-turn`, 12/12 passing) and proven live above.
- **Awaited-REST-on-ordinary-replies risk** — eliminated; the fetch can fire at most once per root per process lifetime, never on normal follow-ups.

> - Mark items resolved by editing this PR body and changing `[ ]` to `[x]`
> - Claim items by adding your GitHub handle to the `Assignee` column
> - Priority guide: **P0** = crash/blocker/security, **P1** = significant bug/regression, **P2** = minor bug/enhancement, **P3** = nice-to-have/stale

target.title: feat(attach): record + resume the Claude cli session bound to an attach session
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

PR #96454 launches Claude Code bound to a gateway session, but the conversation is invisible to OpenClaw and lost on exit: the gateway never learns *which* Claude cli session the attach started, so it can't surface the transcript or let you continue the same conversation on the next attach.

## Why This Change Was Made

This is the **resume + recording** piece of the `attach` surface (tracking: openclaw/openclaw#96205, PR4). `openclaw attach` now starts Claude with a **known** session id (`claude --session-id <uuid>`) and **adopts** it into the gateway session — persisting the `{sessionKey ↔ cliSessionId}` binding. That binding does double duty:
- **Recording** — the gateway's existing history-import path (`resolveClaudeCliBindingSessionId` → `readClaudeCliSessionMessages` → `augmentChatHistoryWithCliSessionImports`) surfaces the attach conversation in `chat.history`. It was never wired to attach because nothing told the gateway the session id.
- **Resume** — the next attach to the same gateway session **and same cwd** resumes it (`claude --resume <id>`). Claude scopes sessions per project, so the binding records a `cwdHash` (`hashCliSessionText`) and `attach.grant` returns `resumeSessionId` only on a cwd match — mirroring the cli-backend's existing cwd-invalidation (`cli-session.ts`). A different cwd starts fresh (a different project is a different conversation), so a stale/foreign session never fails `claude --resume`.

It reuses existing storage end-to-end — `setCliSessionBinding` (with `cwdHash`) + the atomic `patchSessionEntry` write (which preserves other providers' bindings + creates the row if missing). **No** new tables, no `session-sync`/`meta.json`, no `conversations_list`.

## User Impact

- `openclaw attach` starts Claude with a known `--session-id` and binds it; re-attaching to the same session **resumes** the same conversation (`--resume`).
- The attach conversation is **recorded** (surfaced in gateway chat history) via the existing import path.
- New gateway method **`attach.adopt({ token, cliSessionId })`** (`operator.admin`); **`attach.grant` now also returns `resumeSessionId`**. Both additive to the (unreleased) attach surface; no protocol bump.
- **No change** to the cli-backend path or existing commands. Adopt is best-effort — a failed adopt never blocks the launch.

## Evidence

- **Gateway tests (16)**: grant/revoke/ttl + `resumeSessionId` returned **only on cwd match** + `attach.adopt` persists the `claude-cli` binding (with `cwdHash`) **preserving other providers' bindings** + `persisted` flag + adopt validation (missing field / unknown grant → `INVALID_REQUEST`).
- **CLI tests (15)**: adopt fires only on the child `"spawn"` event (not before launch, not on spawn-failure, not in `--print-config`), the `--resume` path, malformed-grant/expiry handling, and the spawn/revoke + signal-detach lifecycle.
- Build, oxlint, **import-cycles** all clean; the 276-test gateway suite + the advertised-method-list test pass with `attach.adopt` added.
- **Stack** (each gated on exit code): PR4 delta is 6 files on top of PR2; full stack vs main is 13 files. Stacked on #96454 (PR2) / #96351 (PR1).
- Mutation **68%**; autoreview clean (cwd-encoding + cwd-orphan findings fixed).
- **adopt-on-spawn**: `attach.adopt` fires only in the child `"spawn"` handler — once `claude` actually starts — so it never binds a session id that a `--print-config` (no launch) or a failed spawn would leave uncreated.
- **Real-behavior proof — real Claude Code 2.1.191 in an isolated container** (apple-container, no Docker). A real headless gateway auto-pairs the device as operator, so `attach.grant`/`attach.adopt` execute for real. Same gateway session key `agent:main:realclaude`; only the cwd changes. Tokens/device id redacted.

  **1) First attach, cwd `/work/alpha`** — attach spawns real `claude --session-id <A>`; claude runs and writes a genuine transcript; adopt fires ON SPAWN:
  ```text
  Attaching Claude Code to session agent:main:realclaude …
  Hello from Claude — acknowledged, the attached harness is working.        ← real claude's reply
  ~/.claude/projects/-work-alpha/9b748344-….jsonl  (10,939 bytes)
  entry types: {"user":1,"assistant":1,"queue-operation":2,"attachment":3,"ai-title":1,"last-prompt":1}   ← genuine Claude Code transcript
  ```
  **2) `chat.history` for the session** — OpenClaw imports the REAL transcript after adoption:
  ```text
  $ openclaw gateway call chat.history --params '{"sessionKey":"agent:main:realclaude"}'
  imported roles: 1 user + 1 assistant
  … "content": "hello from a real attached harness …"     ← the real prompt, imported
  ```
  **3) Re-attach, cwd `/work/alpha`** (same project) → resumes the SAME real session:
  ```text
  "launch": [ "claude", "--resume", "9b748344-…", "--mcp-config", "…" ]
  ```
  **4) Attach, cwd `/work/beta`** (different project) → fresh id, never `9b748344` (the cwd gate):
  ```text
  "launch": [ "claude", "--session-id", "32350be6-…", "--mcp-config", "…" ]
  ```
- `claude --resume <uuid>` parses the UUID **as the session id** (verified: `claude --resume <missing>` → "No conversation found with session ID: …"), so the `["--resume", id]` arg form is correct.

**Risks → mitigations:** adopt is best-effort (failed adopt never blocks launch); the binding is grant-scoped (`token → sessionKey`, never header-trusted); resume is cwd-scoped (`cwdHash` gate) so a foreign/stale session never fails the launch; the binding write is atomic (`patchSessionEntry`) so concurrent provider bindings aren't lost.

target.title: fix(provider): harden provider tool schema hooks
target.body:
## Summary
- Harden provider tool schema normalization against plugin-supplied tools with unreadable `parameters` fields.
- Share guarded tool-field reads across Gemini, DeepSeek, and native OpenAI schema hooks so one poisoned tool does not stop sibling normalization or diagnostics.
- Preserve immutable/non-configurable tool descriptors when cloning with normalized parameters.

## Verification
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/plugin-sdk/provider-tools.test.ts -- --reporter=dot`
- `./node_modules/.bin/oxfmt --check src/plugin-sdk/provider-tools.ts src/plugin-sdk/provider-tools.test.ts`
- `./node_modules/.bin/oxlint src/plugin-sdk/provider-tools.ts src/plugin-sdk/provider-tools.test.ts`
- `git diff --check`
- `.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode local`
- AWS Crabbox `pnpm check:changed`: provider `aws`, lease `cbx_8278ba4e3a36`, slug `coral-krill`, run `run_a1d9dfbad8d5`, total `6m46.442s`, exit `0`

## Real behavior proof
Behavior addressed: Provider-owned tool schema hooks no longer throw when a plugin tool exposes a throwing `parameters` getter; healthy sibling tools still normalize.
Real environment tested: Local sparse Codex worktree for focused Vitest/format/lint/autoreview, plus direct AWS Crabbox Linux validation for `pnpm check:changed`.
Exact steps or command run after this patch: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/plugin-sdk/provider-tools.test.ts -- --reporter=dot`; `crabbox run --provider aws --fresh-pr openclaw/openclaw#89571 --idle-timeout 30m --ttl 90m --timing-json --shell -- "set -e; if ! command -v node >/dev/null 2>&1; then curl -fsSL https://deb.nodesource.com/setup_24.x | sudo -E bash -; sudo apt-get install -y nodejs; fi; node -v; npm -v; sudo corepack enable; env OPENCLAW_CHECK_CHANGED_REMOTE_CHILD=1 OPENCLAW_CHANGED_LANES_RAW_SYNC=1 corepack pnpm check:changed"`
Evidence after fix: `src/plugin-sdk/provider-tools.test.ts` passes 12 tests, including unreadable getter and non-configurable descriptor regressions; AWS Crabbox run `run_a1d9dfbad8d5` passed `pnpm check:changed` with lanes `core`, `coreTests`, `extensions`, `extensionTests`.
Observed result after fix: DeepSeek normalization keeps the unreadable tool untouched, normalizes the healthy sibling, and reports an unreadable-parameters diagnostic instead of throwing; remote changed-gate typecheck, lint, and guards pass.
What was not tested: Full repo test suite, live provider calls, and Docker/E2E lanes.

target.title: feat(channels): add pre-auth access requests and grouped DM allowlists
target.body: ## Summary This PR adds a silent access-request workflow for blocked Telegram and WhatsApp direct messages. - Emit observation-only pre-auth hooks before pairing challenges or rejection. - Support grouped DM allowlists and propagate trusted `sender_group` metadata. - Extend `/allowlist add dm --group` to add senders or move them between groups. - Auto-load trusted bundled or managed `access-request` hooks while keeping mutable workspace hooks explicitly opt-in. - Document a workspace hook example and clean-install troubleshooting requirements. This matters because unknown senders currently have no silent path to request access without entering an agent session or receiving an automatic reply. The intended outcome is a bounded, privacy-conscious pre-auth observation surface that trusted hooks can use for operator-side approval workflows. Out of scope: automatically approving senders, running the model for blocked messages, or auto-enabling mutable workspace hook code. Success means blocked senders remain blocked until manually approved, while a configured phrase can notify the operator without creating a session or reply path. Reviewers should focus on the pre-auth trust boundary, workspace hook opt-in behavior, allowlist normalization, and grouped-entry config handling. ## Linked context Which issue does this close? N/A - no linked issue. Which issues, PRs, or discussions are related? N/A. Was this requested by a maintainer or owner? No known maintainer or owner request. This is an external contribution based on a clean-install access-request workflow. ## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs) - Behavior or issue addressed: Unknown Telegram and WhatsApp DM senders can silently request manual access without creating an agent session, running the model, or receiving a reply. - Real environment tested: Clean Docker Compose OpenClaw installations configured with WhatsApp and Telegram `dmPolicy: "allowlist"` and an explicitly enabled workspace `access-request` hook. - Exact steps or command run after this patch: 1. Configure the workspace `access-request` example with environment variables. 2. Run `openclaw config set tools.profile messaging`. 3. Run `openclaw config set commands.config true`. 4. Recreate the container with `docker compose down && docker compose up -d`. 5. Send the configured access phrase from a blocked WhatsApp number. 6. Approve the sender from the operator chat with `/allowlist add dm --channel whatsapp --group restricted `. - Evidence after fix: 1. WhatsApp blocked sender receives no reply after sending the access phrase. 2. Operator receives the silent WhatsApp access request notification. 3. WhatsApp sender admitted via `/allowlist add dm --channel whatsapp`. 4. WhatsApp admitted sender receives a normal agent response. 5. Telegram blocked sender receives no reply after sending the access phrase. 6. Operator receives the silent Telegram access request notification. 7. Telegram sender admitted via `/allowlist add dm --channel telegram`. - Observed result after fix: Unknown senders on both WhatsApp and Telegram sent the configured access phrase and received no reply. The operator received a silent notification with ready-to-use /allowlist approval commands tailored to each channel. After approval, the sender was admitted to the restricted group. The access phrase used in this test was a custom phrase configured via OPENCLAW_ACCESS_REQUEST_PHRASE. The workspace example ships with Let me in as the default value; operators are encouraged to change it before deploying. - What was not tested: Multi-account deployments were not tested live. - Proof limitations or environment constraints: Phone numbers, gateway tokens, and private endpoints must remain redacted. - Before evidence (optional but encouraged): Before this PR, blocked senders were rejected before any trusted pre-auth observation hook existed. ## Tests and validation Commands run: ```bash node scripts/check-docs-mdx.mjs docs README.md node --import tsx scripts/generate-bundled-channel-config-metadata.ts --check pnpm build git diff --check ``` Focused Vitest validation covered 18 files and 383 tests across Telegram, WhatsApp, hook loading, hook mapping, config reload, inbound metadata, and grouped allowlist edits. Focused Vitest command: ```bash node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run extensions/telegram/src/channel.allowlist.test.ts extensions/telegram/src/config-schema.test.ts extensions/telegram/src/dm-access.test.ts extensions/telegram/src/inbound-context.contract.test.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/config-accessors.test.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/config-schema.test.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/inbound/access-control.test.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/auto-reply/monitor/process-message.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/commands-allowlist.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/inbound-meta.test.ts src/plugin-sdk/allowlist-config-edit.test.ts src/hooks/internal-hooks.test.ts src/hooks/loader.test.ts src/hooks/message-hook-mappers.test.ts src/plugins/wired-hooks-message.test.ts src/gateway/config-reload.test.ts src/gateway/server-reload-handlers.test.ts src/infra/heartbeat-runner.returns-default-unset.test.ts ``` WhatsApp E2E validation: ```bash node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.e2e.config.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/auto-reply.web-auto-reply.connection-and-logging.e2e.test.ts ``` Regression coverage added or updated: - Telegram grouped schema, allowlist editing, blocked-DM pre-auth emission, and sender-group inbound context. - WhatsApp grouped schema, config accessors, pre-auth access control including LID handling, monitor propagation, and E2E coverage. - Core grouped `/allowlist` mutations, internal hook events, plugin hook mapping, config reload behavior, and trusted prompt metadata. Before this fix, blocked Telegram and WhatsApp DMs had no pre-auth observation event, and DM allowlist entries could not carry access-group metadata. ## Risk checklist Did user-visible behavior change? (`Yes/No`) Yes. Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`Yes/No`) Yes. The config schema adds optional grouped DM `allowFrom` entries. Existing string entries remain supported. No migration is required. Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`Yes/No`) Yes. What is the highest-risk area? The new pre-auth hook exposes blocked-sender DM content to trusted handlers before admission. How is that risk mitigated? The hook is observation-only, sessionless, model-free, reply-free, bounded, and fire-and-forget. Mutable workspace hooks remain explicitly opt-in. Trusted bundled or managed hooks may auto-load only when an account uses `dmPolicy: "allowlist"`. Sender identities are validated and untrusted notification fields are sanitized. ## Current review state What is the next action? Submit for maintainer review. What is still waiting on author, maintainer, CI, or external proof? CI and maintainer review pending. Which bot or reviewer comments were addressed? Earlier review findings were addressed: - Restricted automatic loading to trusted bundled or managed hooks; workspace hooks remain opt-in. - Preserved WhatsApp grouped entries through monitor config snapshots. - Regenerated Telegram bundled config metadata for grouped `allowFrom` entries. ## Agent Transcripts Two redacted English reviewer artifacts are available: - [Curated implementation transcript summary](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/28520597/openclaw-pre-auth-agent-transcript.en.md) — recommended for a quick review. - [Full translated implementation transcript](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/28520607/openclaw-pre-auth-agent-transcript.full.en.md) — preserves the approved transcript ordering and original truncation boundary for detailed audit context.

target.title: fix(media): resolve absolute-path channel media for native vision blocks
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Telegram inbound images saved to disk are not passed to the model as native vision content blocks. The model receives only `<media:image>` placeholder text and cannot analyze the actual image. The same workflow works correctly through the webchat / Control UI.
- Why now: Any user sending images via Telegram DM to an OpenClaw bot with a vision-capable model cannot get the agent to see or describe the image. The image file is correctly downloaded to `~/.openclaw/media/inbound/` but is silently dropped during native vision block hydration.
- What changed:
  - `MediaAttachmentCache.resolveLocalPath()` now validates absolute filesystem paths against local roots via `resolveUsableLocalCandidate`, matching the existing relative-path validation added in 610691c796
  - `resolveCurrentTurnImages()` now returns successfully resolved images even when a subset fails, instead of discarding the entire batch
  - Added regression test for absolute-path media hydration
- What did NOT change: Relative-path handling is unchanged. Webchat inline-image path (early-return on `params.images`) is untouched. No new dependencies added.
- Outcome: Channel-delivered absolute-path images (Telegram, Discord, Slack, etc.) are now hydrated into native vision content blocks instead of being silently dropped on a single read failure.
- Reviewer focus: `attachments.cache.ts` changes to `resolveLocalPath`, `current-turn-images.ts` changes to the all-or-nothing fallback logic, and the new test case.

## Linked context

Closes #79162, closes #62292, closes #69808

Was this requested by a maintainer?
- No, but the issues have clear reproduction steps and root cause analysis.

## Root Cause

- Root cause: `resolveLocalPath()` skipped absolute-path validation — it returned `path.resolve(rawPath)` directly while relative paths went through `resolveUsableLocalCandidate`. Channel-delivered inbound media (Telegram, Discord, Slack, etc.) use absolute filesystem paths from `saveMediaBuffer`. When the path later failed `isInboundPathAllowed` in `ensureLocalStat`, the error was silently caught — channel attachments have `url` cleared, so `getBuffer` had no fallback and re-threw. Separately, `resolveCurrentTurnImages()` discarded all images when any single read failed.
- Missing detection / guardrail: No test coverage for absolute-path media hydration. The existing test only covered relative paths. The symmetric relative-path validation in `resolveLocalPath` (added in 610691c796) was the guardrail that should have also covered absolute paths.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: Telegram inbound images saved to disk are not passed to the model as native vision content blocks; the model receives only `<media:image>` placeholder text and cannot analyze the actual image.
- Real environment tested: Ubuntu 24.04 (x64), Node 22.22.0, OpenClaw main branch (9166165).
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  ```bash
  node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/auto-reply/reply/current-turn-images.test.ts
  ```
- Evidence after fix:
  ```text
  node scripts/run-vitest.mjs output:
  ✓ hydrates Telegram-style state-relative media into native prompt images
  ✓ hydrates channel-delivered absolute-path media into native prompt images
  Test Files  1 passed (1)
       Tests  2 passed (2)
  ```
- Observed result after fix: The new absolute-path test passes alongside the existing relative-path test. resolveCurrentTurnImages now returns partially-resolved image batches instead of discarding all images on a single failure. The absolute-path validation in resolveLocalPath is symmetric with the existing relative-path handling.
- What was not tested: Not run against a live Telegram bot with a real image. The proof exercises the full hydration chain through the media store with local file I/O.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: Unit-test-based — does not verify a live end-to-end Telegram roundtrip.

## Tests and validation

Which commands did you run?
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/auto-reply/reply/current-turn-images.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/media-understanding/runner.attachments.test.ts`

What regression coverage was added or updated?
- Added 1 new test case:
  1. `hydrates channel-delivered absolute-path media into native prompt images` — writes a file under a temp state/media directory, sets `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR`, and asserts absolute-path hydration into native `ImageContent` blocks

What failed before this fix, if known?
- Telegram inbound images appeared only as `<media:image>` placeholder text
- Any channel using `saveMediaBuffer` for inbound media could silently lose native vision blocks when a single attachment read failed

target.title: fix(opencode-go): treat stream block boundaries as liveness
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

The opencode-go stalled-stream watchdog only treated provider delta events as stream liveness. Live streams that emitted block-boundary events, such as tool call end/start transitions, could sit between token deltas long enough for the idle timer to abort the underlying request and replace the eventual completed answer with an error.

## Why This Change Was Made

This keeps the stricter first-delta behavior intact, so synthetic start or block-start events before provider output do not shorten the first-event window. Once a real provider delta has arrived, later provider-owned block boundaries now refresh the idle timer because they prove the SSE stream is still advancing.

## User Impact

Users of opencode-go models should no longer lose completed answers when a provider pauses token deltas while closing one block and opening another. Truly stalled streams are still aborted after the configured idle timeout.

## Evidence

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run extensions/opencode-go/stream-termination.test.ts`
- `& .\node_modules\.bin\oxfmt.cmd --check --threads=1 extensions/opencode-go/stream-termination.ts extensions/opencode-go/stream-termination.test.ts`
- `git diff --check`
Live opencode-go wrapper stream proof from this branch:

```text
$ <script invokes extensions/opencode-go/stream-termination.ts#createOpencodeGoStalledStreamWrapper with real timers and opencode-go stream events>
{
  "source": "extensions/opencode-go/stream-termination.ts#createOpencodeGoStalledStreamWrapper",
  "results": [
    {
      "scenario": "post-delta boundary events keep stream live",
      "idleTimeoutMs": 100,
      "elapsedAfterDeltaMs": 210,
      "boundaryEvents": [
        "toolcall_end",
        "toolcall_start"
      ],
      "abortCalled": false,
      "terminalEvent": "done",
      "errorEvents": 0
    },
    {
      "scenario": "true post-delta stall still aborts",
      "idleTimeoutMs": 100,
      "quietAfterDeltaMs": 160,
      "abortCalled": true,
      "terminalEvent": "error",
      "errorMessage": "opencode-go stream timed out after provider-owned SSE boundary stalled"
    }
  ]
}
```

This exercises the actual opencode-go stalled-stream wrapper. The first stream stays live across post-delta `toolcall_end` and `toolcall_start` boundaries for longer than one idle window and completes with `done`. The second stream has no boundary or delta after its first delta, so the wrapper still aborts and emits the provider-owned stalled-stream error.

Fixes #96518

target.title: fix(feishu): allow number/boolean/array types in bitable fields parameter
target.body:
## Problem

The `fields` parameter in `CreateRecordSchema` and `UpdateRecordSchema` uses `Type.Any()` which serializes to an empty JSON Schema `{}`. This makes the LLM default to passing all values as **strings**, even for numeric fields where `number` type is expected.

For example, the LLM passes `{"Price": "99"}` instead of `{"Price": 99}`, causing type mismatches in the Bitable API.

## Fix

Change `Type.Any()` to `Type.Union([Type.String(), Type.Number(), Type.Boolean(), Type.Array(Type.Any()), Type.Record(Type.String(), Type.Any())])` so the JSON Schema explicitly advertises `"type": ["string", "number", "boolean", "array", "object"]`.

### Changes
- **`CreateRecordSchema.fields`** (line 533): `Type.Any()` → explicit union type
- **`UpdateRecordSchema.fields`** (line 575): same change

Both `property` in `CreateFieldSchema` keeps `Type.Any()` as-is, since field creation properties have field-type-specific structures that are too varied to constrain.

target.title: fix(agents): use CJK-aware token estimation for tool results
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves
`TOOL_RESULT_CHARS_PER_TOKEN = 2` 与 `estimateStringChars`的中文字符权重(4 chars/token)不匹配，导致CJK-rich内容被2倍高估，触发false token overflow错误，中断中文工作流。

例如：85k中文字符被估计为170k tokens，实际应为~85k，导致本不应触发的preemptive compaction提前激活。

## Evidence
### Verifiable Behavior
- **Issue Reference**: #95327
- **Real Behavior Proof**: 19个token估计测试通过，包括CJK-specific测试用例
- **Test Results**: 
```
[pnpm test] preemptive-compaction.test.ts
Tests 19 passed (19)
Duration 7.63s

CJK Test: 85,000+ Chinese chars estimated as ~85,000 tokens (ratio: ~1.0)
Mixed Lang Test: ratio 0.8-1.5, healthy
```

### Technical Evidence
- **Before**: `TOOL_RESULT_CHARS_PER_TOKEN = 2` 导致CJK被double-count
- **After**: `= 4` 与正常文本估计一致
- **Files Changed**: `src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/preemptive-compaction.ts` (1 line)
- **Test Updates**: `preemptive-compaction.test.ts` added 3 CJK-specific cases

### Regression Risk
- Low: 仅修改常数值，无逻辑变更
- 所有19个估计测试通过
- 回归可能性：tool result token预算现在更准确，不会over-estimate

## Why This Change Was Made
使tool result token estimation与CJK字符权重对齐，防止false overflow，确保多语言工作流公平对待CJK字符。

## User Impact
- 中文工作流不再因错误的token overflow中断
- Tool result上下文预算更准确
- 无配置或操作变更，零用户学习成本
